summary: Jeff Atwood highlights Bruce Tognazzini's list of the 10 most wanted UI design bugs, which catalogs persistent usability problems in graphical interfaces. Tognazzini, considered the father of the Macintosh UI, identifies issues ranging from power failure data loss to focus-stealing dialogs. While Atwood acknowledges that a couple items read as personal complaints about Apple design decisions, he agrees the majority represent genuinely frustrating UI problems that plague every GUI. The post also points readers to Marissa Mayer's perspective on Google's contrasting approach to user experience design.
key_points: Tognazzini's list identifies universal GUI problems that persist across platforms, not just Apple-specific issues
Focus-stealing dialogs, mysteriously dimmed menu items, and ASCII sort bugs remain unsolved decades into GUI computing
Some criticism in the list reflects personal design disagreements rather than objective usability flaws
The post contrasts traditional UI expertise (Tognazzini) with Google's emerging approach to user experience
money_quotes: If the computer loses power for more than a few thousandths of a second, it throws everything away.
Designers offer no way for users to discover why a given menu or option has been dimmed (grayed out), nor how to turn it back on.
15 Dec 2008 sorts as being before 2 Jan 1900.

You're working on a multitasking system, typing away merrily in window A. Meanwhile, some background task decides it needs your attention, pops up a dialog, and moves the keyboard input focus from the window you were working on to its dialog box.
Two of these come across as cranky 'I coulda done it better' complaints about specific Apple design decisions he doesn't agree with. But the rest are truly aggravating UI problems in every GUI.
key_insight: Many fundamental UI design bugs identified decades ago remain unsolved in modern software, representing persistent failures in how we build graphical interfaces.
